title: Geography & Environment
section_title_intro: Geography isn't just a map — it's the landscape that shapes everything else. Mountains create borders that become political divisions. Deserts isolate cultures. Rivers determine where cities grow. Control of fertile valleys determines who holds power.

content.text: In This Section
Concepts & Frameworks
Part One: The Foundation
Part Two: Geography & Romance
This section is divided into two parts:

**Part One: The Foundation** — The physical reality of your world. Terrain, climate, resources, dangers, and how geography shapes where and how people live. This is the geographic baseline that exists regardless of your characters' romantic situation.

**Part Two: Geography & Romance** — How physical geography creates romantic tension. Separation and proximity, dangerous journeys together, entering each other's worlds, places that hold emotional meaning.
You need both.
A Note Before You Begin
Not every question will apply to your story. **Skip what doesn't fit.**

**Worldbuild for its own sake if you want.** A richly developed world has value beyond what appears on the page. Use the "romance connection" prompts for brainstorming if they're helpful, or leave them blank if that element is just background texture.
**Come back later.** If you're not sure whether something matters yet, skip it and return once your story has taken more shape.
This workbook is a tool, not a test.
How to Think About Geography in Romantasy

Geography in romantasy functions as a **pressure system** — the physical world can compress your characters together, force them apart, or create conditions where they must rely on each other. Before you draw a map, ask yourself: *What geographic situation does my romance need?*

Not every hill and river needs to serve the plot. But the geography you emphasize — the locations where scenes happen, the terrain your characters traverse, the distances between them — should be doing something. Background geography creates atmosphere and believability. Foreground geography creates story.

content_blocks.text: Develop geography that creates friction — between your characters, or between what they want and what they can have.
What physical obstacles keep them apart?

items: An ocean that takes months to cross
A magical barrier that can only be crossed under certain conditions
War zones that make travel deadly

content_blocks.text: What forces them together despite themselves?

items: A ship voyage with no escape
Water rights in a desert region
The only pass through the mountains
A rare magical resource found in contested territory
Trade routes that require cooperation between enemies

content.text: Geography that doesn't create friction isn't wasted — it's just setting.

content.title: The "Pressure Cooker" Principle
content.question: The most effective romantasy settings function as pressure cookers — environments that trap characters together and force interaction. When characters can't escape each other, they're forced to confront conflict instead of avoiding it, rely on each other despite distrust, and share space long enough for attraction to build beneath their defenses.

content.text: Reference: Place Name Patterns
Common English Place Name Patterns
Mix prefixes and suffixes to generate names. "Ash" + "ford" = Ashford. "Black" + "moor" = Blackmoor.
Nordic/Scandinavian Place Name Patterns
Good for cold, northern, Viking-inspired settings. Old Norse roots appear in many English place names too.
Celtic/Welsh Place Name Patterns
Good for mystical, ancient, fae-adjacent settings. Celtic names often describe landscape features poetically.
Slavic Place Name Patterns
Good for Eastern European-inspired settings. Slavic names often use descriptive compounds.
Your World's Place Name Patterns
Create prefixes and suffixes that fit your world's languages. What sounds right for this culture?
The Big Picture: Your World's Scale
What's the geographic scope of your story?
Check the scope that best fits your story, then note the primary locations where your romance actually takes place.

example_rows: Thornwick Academy — a remote magical school on sea cliffs where students are trapped together for the term

content.text: If your world is large, where specifically does your romance happen?
You don't need to detail everything — just locations that matter.

example_rows: The Winter Court — an ice palace where protagonist is held hostage, forced into proximity with the prince

content.text: Kingdoms & Nations Overview
This is reference material. Fill in what you know now; come back as your world develops.
Terrain Types
Only fill in terrains that matter. If your characters never see a desert, skip it.
What terrains shape your story?

example_rows: Natural defense, mineral deposits, isolation for monasteries
Impassable in winter, separates lovers from different kingdoms, treacherous passes

content.text: Resources & Territorial Control
Reference: Natural Resource Ideas
Use this list for inspiration when filling out the table below.

rows: Gold, silver, copper, iron ore, bronze, platinum, mithril
Diamonds, rubies, sapphires, emeralds, pearls, amber, jade, obsidian, marble
Silk, wool, linen, cotton, rare furs, leather, spider-silk, feathers
Grain, rice, olives, wine/grapes, sugar, honey, salt, spices, rare fruits
Ivory, bone, whale oil, animal fat, beeswax, dyes from shells, ambergris
Timber, herbs, medicinal plants, rare flowers, resins, incense, tea, coffee
Coal, oil, charcoal, peat, magical crystals, dragon-fire, captured lightning
Ley line access, enchanted springs, magical creatures, spell components
Fossils, adder stones, meteoric iron, ancient artifacts, bottled starlight

content.text: What natural resources are geographically concentrated?
Think beyond the obvious. What does this world have that ours doesn't? What scarcity shapes daily life?

example_rows: River valley between the two kingdoms
Contested — marriage alliance proposed to end the conflict

content.text: What geographic resource creates tension or conflict in your world?
Water rights? Arable land? Trade routes? Does the conflict show up as war, political marriage, trade embargo, or something subtler?
How does geographic resource control affect your characters?
Does one character's family/nation control something the other's needs? Is there territorial dispute between their peoples?

content.note: You've mapped where resources are found and who controls them. In Section 4: Resources & Scarcity, you'll explore how resource control creates economic stakes, cultural traditions, and romantic tension.

content.text: The Sensory World
What does your primary setting look, sound, smell, and feel like?
Close your eyes and walk through this place. What hits your senses first? What would your protagonist notice?

rows: What the air feels like (temperature, humidity, wind)

content.text: How does the environment change at different times of day?
Dawn meetings feel different than midnight ones. What time of day would be most romantic here? Most dangerous?
What's beautiful about this place?
The details a character falling in love would notice. What would make someone homesick for this place?
What's harsh or uncomfortable about this place?
What do people endure or complain about? What takes getting used to?
Climate, Weather & Seasons
What's the overall climate?
Tropical, temperate, arid, polar? How does it shape what people wear, build, and grow?
What seasons exist and how distinct are they?
Traditional four seasons? A long winter and brief summer? Magical seasons tied to something other than the sun?
What season is your story set in?
Seasons create mood and constraint. What does this season make possible for your romance? What does it prevent?
How do seasons affect life in your world?
When can armies march? When do ships sail? When are weddings held? When must everyone stay indoors?
Does the season create pressure or opportunity in your story?
Racing against winter? Waiting for spring thaw? Only able to travel in summer?
Dangerous Regions
What regions are dangerous or forbidden?
Every world has places people warn you not to go. What makes them deadly? What would force your characters there anyway?

example_rows: Corrupted magic, monsters, no water for miles, disorienting mists
Exiles, desperate smugglers, and our protagonists who must cross it to reach sanctuary

content.text: Do your characters travel through dangerous territory?
Shared danger bonds people fast. What threats would force them to rely on each other?
Borders & Boundaries
What geographic features create borders?
Mountain ranges? Rivers? Oceans? Magical barriers? Deserts?
How permeable are these borders?
Easy to cross? Heavily guarded? Physically impassable? Seasonally accessible?
Do your characters come from opposite sides of a significant border?
Borders create "us" and "them." What assumptions would each character have about someone from the other side?
Home & Belonging
Where does your protagonist feel at home?
The place that shaped them, where they feel most themselves:
What does home look, smell, sound like to them?
The sensory details that mean "home":
Where does your love interest feel at home?
The place that shaped them, where they're most themselves. How different is it from your protagonist's home?
Where does each character feel like an outsider?
Fish-out-of-water moments reveal character. Where would each feel uncertain, uncomfortable, or judged?
Entering Each Other's Worlds
Does one character visit the other's home territory?
Bringing someone home is an act of vulnerability. What would they be proud to show? What would they rather hide?
What's the sensory experience of entering unfamiliar territory?
What do they notice first? What's strange? What's beautiful? What's uncomfortable?
How does the "native" character see their home differently through the visitor's eyes?
Pride? Shame? New appreciation? Defensiveness?
What does the visitor understand about their partner after seeing where they come from?
Place explains people. What becomes clear about your love interest once you see where they grew up?
Travel & Distance
How do people typically travel?
On foot? Horseback? Ships? Flying creatures? Magical transportation?
Travel Time Reference
These are real-world estimates. Feel free to adjust for your world.
How long does travel take between key locations?
Distance creates stakes. A week apart feels different than a year. How painful is separation in your world?

rows: From one character's home to the other's

content.text: Do your characters undertake a significant journey?
The road is where walls come down. What would a long journey together reveal about each of them?
Isolated Locations
What isolated locations exist?
Islands, remote estates, mountain fortresses, ships at sea, academies, pocket dimensions:

example_rows: Political prisoners sent there; protagonist exiled after scandal
Mountain passes are snowed in 8 months/year; magic wards prevent unauthorized departure

content.text: Geography as Emotional Landscape
What's the emotional quality of your primary setting?
Does it feel oppressive, expansive, peaceful, threatening, beautiful, decaying?
Is there a location that becomes meaningful to your characters specifically?
A place they return to, a place where something changed, a place that's "theirs":
Integration Check

rows: How does geography explain cultural differences? (Reference: Section 3)
How does geography create or reinforce power structures? (Reference: Section 5)
How does geography interact with magic? (Reference: Section 8)
What geographic resource or territory is contested in your world?

content.text: Quick Reference: Geographic Stakes

example_rows: The Winter Court — where protagonist is hostage and love interest is prince
rows: The barrier separating peoples or characters

content.text: Map Making: What to Include on Your Map
Consider which of these appear in your world. You don't need all of them.

rows: Mountains, rivers, forests, lakes, coastlines, islands, deserts, swamps, valleys
Kingdom borders, disputed territories, neutral zones, conquered lands
Capitals, port cities, border towns, villages, abandoned settlements
Major roads, trade routes, mountain passes, river crossings, ports
Castles, walls, watchtowers, military camps, fortresses
Temples, shrines, pilgrimage routes, sacred groves, forbidden places
Ley lines, magical academies, cursed regions, power sources
Where characters meet, where they're forbidden to go, where they flee to
